text:
*[Bug 19011] Fix for cdplot returning probabilities greater than One*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19011>

--- Comment #4 from Achim Zeileis (<mailto:Achim.Zeileis@r-project.org|Achim.Zeileis@r-project.org>) ---
Christoph, thanks for pointing out the problem and suggesting the fix. I agree
that both aspects (density computation, NaN handling) are sensible
improvements.

Following your ideas I have implemented the fix a bit differently:

- I avoid computing the conditional densities for all categories **and** the
corresponding cumultative densities. I only compute the former and then use
cumulative sums to obtain the latter. This also makes sure that the densities
are indeed ordered, even in low-density regions affected by the problems you
point out.

- Rather than removing the NaNs _after_ computing them, I remove the
zero-density x-coordinates _before_ the NaNs occur in the subsequent
observations.

I checked that the fixed version of `cdplot.default()` still yields the same
results as the previous version when there are no zero-density intervals. Also,
my fixed version provides the same output as Christoph's fixed version on his
example.

--- Comment #11 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
I also find some r-devel messages in later months that were not captured yet in
this thread, copying for further context:

<https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2008-January/048063.html>

----

Dear all,

regarding the bug in pacf
I have prepared a patch (attached) and tested it against the latest R devel

x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu

44040
language
R

r44040)


Here I reproduce the example I gave in my previous post to trigger the bug

Partial autocorrelations of series 'ts(cbind(x, y))', by lag

, , x

x            y
 0.049 (  1)  0.002 ( -1)
 0.012 (  2) -0.072 ( -2)
 0.002 (  3)  0.036 ( -3)
 0.014 (  4)  0.013 ( -4)
 0.051 (  5)  0.003 ( -5)
 0.033 (  6) -0.018 ( -6)
 0.027 (  7)  0.069 ( -7)
-0.027 (  8)  0.028 ( -8)
-0.010 (  9) -0.015 ( -9)
-0.013 ( 10) -0.015 (-10)

, , y

x            y
 0.041 (  1) -0.045 (  1)
 0.008 (  2) -0.027 (  2)
-0.056 (  3) -0.030 (  3)
 0.013 (  4) -0.020 (  4)
 0.017 (  5)  0.004 (  5)
-0.021 (  6) -0.010 (  6)
 0.022 (  7)  0.061 (  7)
 0.029 (  8) -0.034 (  8)
-0.014 (  9)  0.031 (  9)
-0.002 ( 10)  0.019 ( 10)

below you can find relevant info and an example where I reproduce Wei's book
results.

<http://www2.stat.unibo.it/giannerini/R/pacf.htm>

The routine is pure R, on the website there is also a F95 version.
I am willing to write a FORTRAN 77 version of the patch if needed but I
think this might replace the bugged routine temporarily. Let me know.

Thanks for your attention

Simone

text:
*[Bug 10455] Bug in pacf -- Proposed patch*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=10455>

--- Comment #12 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
Condensing the above. TL;DR: `pacf()` is confirmed buggy because it can easily
produce "correlations" exceeding 1 in magnitude.

The reprex is paraphrased here:

```
set.seed(10)

x <- rnorm(1000,sd=10000)
y <- rnorm(1000,sd=1)
z <- pacf(ts(cbind(x,y)),plot=FALSE,lag.max=10)
z$acf[,"x","y", drop=FALSE]
# , , y
#             x
# 1   374.05174
# 2    66.71705
# 3  -535.80950
# 4   120.80163
# 5   142.82444
# 6  -211.71098
# 7   201.85576
# 8   286.07852
# 9  -134.05655
# 10  -18.20016
```

`acf(type="partial")` does give the identical result:

```
acf(ts(cbind(x,y)), type='partial', plot=FALSE,
lag.max=10)$acf[,"x","y",drop=FALSE]
# , , y
#             x
# 1   374.05174
# 2    66.71705
# 3  -535.80950
# 4   120.80163
# 5   142.82444
# 6  -211.71098
# 7   201.85576
# 8   286.07852
# 9  -134.05655
# 10  -18.20016
```

There's some ambiguity about whether `pacf()` is intended for "correlation" or
"autoregression"; as of today, the documentation _does_ explicitly say
["correlation"](<https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/blob/5a018329613b1f8fc09ca069a336d928ac3b4c25/src/library/stats/man/acf.Rd#L15-L16>),
To that end, Prof. Giannerini provides some patches based on the Wei text (I
don't have access; the Wikipedia page does cite some of the other texts listed
here: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partial_autocorrelation_function>, as well
as mentioning the
[Durbin](<https://repository.lib.ncsu.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/574e3264-2014-487c-9129-37ae4bea7d9e/content>)-Levinson
algorithm. I belive I _do_ have Shumway-Stoffer at home somewhere...). Internet
Archive did not manage to crawl those patch files unfortunately. I e-mailed
Prof. Giannerini requesting the patches.

As to "correlation" vs. "autoregression", possibly that could be a new option
to `pacf()`, possibly with the default being `"autoregression"` if there's some
value to back-compatibility here.

text:
*[Bug 16166] inconsistent results of zapsmall called by Thin.row (especially in anova.mlm)*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16166>

Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |michaelchirico4@gmail.com

--- Comment #5 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
Making the above more self-contained for readers like myself lacking further
context:

```
reacttime <- matrix(c(
420, 420, 480, 480, 600, 780,
420, 480, 480, 360, 480, 600,
480, 480, 540, 660, 780, 780,
420, 540, 540, 480, 780, 900,
540, 660, 540, 480, 660, 720,
360, 420, 360, 360, 480, 540,
480, 480, 600, 540, 720, 840,
480, 600, 660, 540, 720, 900,
540, 600, 540, 480, 720, 780,
480, 420, 540, 540, 660, 780),
ncol = 6, byrow = TRUE,
dimnames = list(subj = 1:10,
              cond = c("deg0NA", "deg4NA", "deg8NA",
                       "deg0NP", "deg4NP", "deg8NP")))

mlmfit <- lm(reacttime ~ 1)
mlmfit0 <- update(mlmfit, ~0)

idata <- data.frame(deg = gl(3, 1, 6, labels = c(0, 4, 8)),
                    noise = gl(2, 3, 6, labels = c("A", "P")))

anova(mlmfit, mlmfit0, idata=idata, X=~deg*noise, M=~deg*noise)
# Error in abs(sapply(deltassd, function(X) diag((T %*% X %*% t(T))))) : 
#   non-numeric argument to mathematical function
```

`X` and `M` denote the "inner" and "outer" projection matrices, while `T` is
the "transformation" matrix given by default by `Thin.row(Proj(M) - Proj(X))`.
(NB: I'm not very famililar with ANOVA, nor with MLM).

And then Peter's `if (Rank(cbind(M, X)) != Rank(M))` is from `anova.mlmlist`:

<https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/blob/5a018329613b1f8fc09ca069a336d928ac3b4c25/src/library/stats/R/mlm.R#L426-L427>

----

Adding to this my support for Peter's idea to check `Rank(X) < Rank(M)`, i.e.,
that the inner space is strictly smaller than the outer one. Basically we want
`span(X) ⊂ span(M)` where now we only enforce `span(X) ⊆ span(M)`.

`Rank()` also uses `zapsmall()`, but it does so on the scale of `X` and `M`
themselves, thus less susceptible to the problem identified of `zapsmall()`
latching on to the largest infinitesimal value.
